question: How do the occupancies of the 24d and 96h sites in the doped structures compare to the experimental values mentioned in the text?
answer: The occupancies of the 24d and 96h sites in the doped structures (0.458 for 24d sites and 0.406/0.427 for 96h sites) are similar to the experimental values of 0.564 and 0.442 for these sites, respectively. This similarity indicates that the doped structures closely match the experimental observations.
sentences: These occupancies are similar to the experimental values of 0.564 and 0.442 for these sites, respectively.12 In the Al-doped structure (Li6.25 Al0.25La3Zr2O12), two randomly selected 24d Li sites were replaced by Al. (Li 24d sites have been previously identiﬁed as the most energetically favorable sites for Al doping.33) To maintain charge neutrality, a total of four Li atoms located near the Al-sites were also removed from the cell. Likewise, for the Ta-doped structure (Li6.5La3Zr1.5Ta0.5O12), four Zr atoms were replaced by an equivalent number of Ta dopants,18 and a total of four Li vacancies were created to maintain charge neutrality. The resulting occupancies of the 24d sites are 0.458 (equivalent to 11 Li+ ions in unit cell) for both the Al- and Ta-doped cells and 0.406 and 0.427 (equivalent to 39 and 41 Li+ ions in unit cell) for the 96h sites, respectively. To facilitate comparisons with the mechanical properties of a Li anode, the elastic properties of metallic BCC lithium were also investigated. The elastic properties of a solid phase having a cubic crystal structure can be described using three elastic constants: C11, C12, and C44.34 The elastic constants were evaluated using Hook’s law by calculating the total energy as a function of strain. In addition to being ingredients in the calculation of the elastic constants constitute fundamental mechanical properties of a crystalline phase.

question: How does the special issue of Next Energy address the topic of aqueous rechargeable batteries?
answer: The special issue of Next Energy, titled 'Current progresses and next generation aqueous electrolyte-based batteries,' covers a broad range of aqueous rechargeable batteries (ARBs) with a specific focus on Zn-based systems. It provides an overview of current advancements and future directions in this field.
